Search results: 



--- SOURCE 1: Self-healing polymers: Synthesis methods and applications ---
URL: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352507X20300470

SUMMARY:
This review will address several aspects such as preparation, structure–property relationships, properties of different types of clays and self-healing polymers, healing process of cracks in clays using polymers, crack healing mechanism, test methods of healing efficiency, properties (e.g. structure, hydraulic conductivity, swelling nature, mechanical and rheology) of clays before and after healing of cracks, and their applications. This review addresses the fabrication methodologies and structural characteristics of different layered clay-based materials, and synthesis strategies of various self-healing polymers, their structure–property relationships. Self-healing process of cracks in different clays through self-healing polymers, crack-healing mechanisms, test methods of healing efficiency, properties (e.g.,hydraulic conductivity, mechanical, and rheological) of crack-healed clays, and their advances in engineering areas are discussed.

Self-healing polymers: Synthesis methods and applications
=========================================================

Author links open overlay panel[Kakarla Raghava Reddy a c](https://www.sciencedirect.com/author/13611741600/raghava-reddy-kakarla), Abbas El-Zein b, David W.Airey b, Fernando Alonso-Marroquin b, Peter Schubel c, Allan Manalo c

Abstract
--------

Clays have numerous applications in a variety of applications including engineering, medical and civil areas. The wide applicability of clays is attributed to their useful physico-mechanical as well as biomedical applications, since they are readily available, nontoxic to human body and environment, low cost and having kneading, swelling, adsorption of properties for metal ions. Industrial sectors in different fields are greatly dependent on the usage of different clays, but the cracks or defects or damaged areas present in clays restrict their practical applications. In order to overcome these problems, various polymeric materials have been developed through different strategies to heal the cracks or repair the damaged areas of clays, and improve their properties and potential usage in various applications. This review will address several aspects such as preparation, structure–property relationships, properties of different types of clays and self-healing polymers, healing process of cracks in clays using polymers, crack healing mechanism, test methods of healing efficiency, properties (e.g. structure, hydraulic conductivity, swelling nature, mechanical and rheology) of clays before and after healing of cracks, and their applications.

*   [Previous article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352507X20300421)
*   [Next article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352507X20300330)

Keywords
--------

Clays

Crack healing

Self-healing polymers

RAFT

ATRP

Click chemistry

1. Introduction
---------------

Clays are widely used inorganic materials owing to their low cost, nontoxicity, ready availability in nature, good thermal and environmental stability, hardness, and excellent rheological properties. They have a wide range of potential applications in different research fields and industries such as medicine, cosmetics, steel making, ceramics, hydraulic engineering, infrastructural engineering, construction and building materials, surfacing roads, roofing, flooring, as single liners for canals or ponds, and adsorption and removal of heavy metal ions and hazardous compounds from contaminated water[[1]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352507X20300470#b1), [[2]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352507X20300470#b2), [[3]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352507X20300470#b3), [[4]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352507X20300470#b4). However, the presence of cracks in clays is a major issue that hinders their use for practical applications. Crack healing is important, because small cracks generate [brittleness](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/brittleness "Learn more about brittleness from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") that causes deterioration in their properties[[5]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352507X20300470#b5), [[6]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352507X20300470#b6), [[7]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352507X20300470#b7). Therefore, healing of cracks in damaged areas of clays has been an important topic of research in recent years.

Smart self-healing polymers have received much interest in recent years, owing to their ability to heal damaged material via scratching, or retaining their original physico-chemical and mechanical properties[[8]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352507X20300470#b8), [[9]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352507X20300470#b9), [[10]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352507X20300470#b10). For developing well-defined [organic polymers](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/organic-polymer "Learn more about organic polymers from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") with self-healing properties, the polymers must have functionalities that can react with other groups. This necessitates the use of controlled polymerization reactions by combining suitable coupling reactions, such as embedding organic monomer molecules or catalysts or crosslinking agents such as organic monomers or catalysts or crosslinking agents into [polymer matrix](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/polymer-matrix "Learn more about polymer matrix from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")[[11]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352507X20300470#b11), [[12]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352507X20300470#b12), [[13]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352507X20300470#b13), [[14]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352507X20300470#b14), [[15]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352507X20300470#b15). Among many self-healing methodologies, [covalent bond](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/covalent-bond "Learn more about covalent bond from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") formation is an effective approach for long-standing structural applications, because covalent linkages are quite strong between the healing agent and matrix, giving materials with remarkable [strength](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/mechanical-strength "Learn more about strength from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). The healing of cracks, defects, or damages in clays by polymers is therefore important to improve the reliability and lifetime of clay minerals. To the best of our knowledge, there are no review reports focusing on the effects of cracks, as well as strategies used in healing/repairing cracks in clays.

This review addresses the fabrication methodologies and structural characteristics of different layered clay-based materials, and synthesis strategies of various self-healing polymers, their structure–property relationships. Self-healing process of cracks in different clays through self-healing polymers, crack-healing mechanisms, test methods of healing efficiency, properties (e.g.,hydraulic conductivity, mechanical, and rheological) of crack-healed clays, and their advances in engineering areas are discussed.

2. Classification and properties of clays
-----------------------------------------

Clay particles are inorganic species. Two types of clays as cationic clays, which are layers of [aluminosilicates](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/aluminosilicate "Learn more about aluminosilicates from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") with negative charge, and anionic clays, which are hydroxide layers with positive charge. Based on the elemental composition, the primary component of clay is [aluminum](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/aluminum "Learn more about aluminum from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") silicate, and the secondary components are [apatite](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/apatite "Learn more about apatite from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), zircon, granite, and others. Some of the primary groups of clay minerals are: (i) [kaolinite](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/kaolinite "Learn more about kaolinite from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (hydrated Al silicate), (ii) haloizit (hydrated Al silicate), (iii) Na-montmorillonite (hydrated silicates of mixed Al, Mg, and Na), (iv) illite (hydrated silicates of mixed Al, Fe, Mg, and Na), and several other types of pristine clays within these groups[[16]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352507X20300470#b16), [[17]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352507X20300470#b17).

Clays play a vital role in industrial sectors due to their unique physical, mechanical, and rheological properties. The transport characteristics within clays could be improved by [delamination](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/delamination "Learn more about delamination from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), which provides channels within the matrix and this occurs by pillaring mediators (silicate, alumina), smaller particles (montmorillonite (MMT)) to obtain nanoporous structured clays, or by the insertion of organic molecules (polymers or surfactants).

### 2.1. Layered double hydroxides (LDHs)

[Layered double hydroxides](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/layered-double-hydroxides "Learn more about Layered double hydroxides from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (LDHs), also known as anionic clay minerals or layered hydroxyl salts, are biocompatible. Hydrotalcite (Mg![Image 11: single bond](https://sdfestaticassets-us-east-1.sciencedirectassets.com/shared-assets/55/entities/sbnd.gif)Al![Image 12: single bond](https://sdfestaticassets-us-east-1.sciencedirectassets.com/shared-assets/55/entities/sbnd.gif)CO 3) is a mineral of this family with a similar isomorphic structure. Because hydrotalcite consists of hydroxycarbonate of magnesium and aluminum, it can be easily crushed into a fine powder. Thin layer-like structure of this clay has the formula of Mg 6 Al 2(OH)6 CO 3 H 2 O. Positive charges in hydrotalcite can be achieved by the replacement of Mg 2+ ions by Al 3＋ ions.

LDHs are normally known as hydrotalcite-like material owing to the isomorphic substitution of Al 3＋ ions. LDHs are two-dimensional (2D) morphological structure-based clay minerals with ordered self-assembling layers that have a controllable volume of the interlayers and variable host/guest structures, which can be fabricated through a layer-by-layer (LBL) assembly process. LDHs have a similar structure to brucite, consisting of layers with positive charge and charge-neutralizing anions, and solvent molecules inside the interlayer galleries. The general divalent cations (M 2+) of LDHs are Mg 2+, Zn 2+, Co 2+, Ca 2＋, Cd 2+, Cu 2+, and Ni 2+, whereas trivalent cations (M 3+) cations are Al 3＋, Cr 3＋, and Fe 3＋. Low-dimensional structured [nanosheets](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/nanosheet "Learn more about nanosheets from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of LDHs are formed by placing of metal cations in the centers of edge-sharing octahedra, where each metallic cation consists of six hydroxyl ions at the corners[[18]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352507X20300470#b18).

The unique and fascinating characteristics of LDHs such as the well-ordered structures, molecular self-aggregation inside the interlayer galleries, high thermal stability, ability to intercalate different types of species (e.g.,inorganic, organic, and biomolecules), and simple LBL fabrication process render them highly attractive for potential applications in optics, separation technology, nanomaterial engineering, medicine, and catalysis. In particular, biocompatible characteristics of LDHs allow the clay to monitor, identify, and treat many diseases in a modestly invasive mode.

### 2.2. Montmorillonite (MMT)

MMT belongs to the smectite clay group, has tremendous intercalation properties, robust adsorption, and elevated affinity to organic molecules and heavy metals owing to its intrinsic inorganic 2D layered [nanostructure](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/nanostructure "Learn more about nanostructure from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), as shown in [Fig.1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352507X20300470#fig1)[[19]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352507X20300470#b19). With a strong pre-concentration ability, high cation exchange capability, and a peculiar layered structure, MMT has attracted much attention. MMT has some unique characteristics such as low price, good stability to chemicals, strong absorption, good [electron transport](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/electron-transfer "Learn more about electron transport from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") properties, and controllable structural properties. A subgroup of MMT is bentonite clay, which has applications in electrochemical sensors for the detection of organic molecules, cations, and adsorbents[[20]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352507X20300470#b20).

![Image 13](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2352507X20300470-gr1.jpg)

### 2.3. Sepiolite

Sepiolite clay (Si 12 O 30 Mg 8(OH,F)4(H 2 O)4.8H 2 O) is a fibrous clay and used as filler to strengthen the polymer mass. They do not have ability to mix, but provide interesting features such as high surface area and microporosity. Its morphological structure consists of octahedral [magnesium oxide](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/magnesia "Learn more about magnesium oxide from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") planes sandwiched between 3D [nanosheets](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/nanosheet "Learn more about nanosheets from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of tetrahedral silicates. The anionic and cationic charges on the surface and between the inner layers provide excellent absorption properties for heavy metal ions and polar organic materials. The presence of hydroxyl groups (Si![Image 14: single bond](https://sdfestaticassets-us-east-1.sciencedirectassets.com/shared-assets/55/entities/sbnd.gif)OH) at the exterior surface permits for easy functioning by the controlled modification via chemical reactions, e.g.,by the attachment of [nanoparticles](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/nanoparticle "Learn more about nanoparticles from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") or with binding agents[[21]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352507X20300470#b21).

### 2.4. Brucite

Brucite is a layer-like structured magnesium hydroxide [Mg(OH)6)], in which divalent M 2+ions are octahedrally bounded by the hydroxide ions. These octahedral units share their edges and generate infinite layers in which hydroxyl (OH) form chemical bonds to the nanolayers. These OH anions are well-ordered in a highly packed 2D structured planes. The octahedral holes of hydroxyl planes are conquered by [magnesium ions](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/magnesium-ion "Learn more about magnesium ions from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), resulting in a neutral layer. The elemental composition can change structure of brucite clay through the isomorphic replacement of divalent cations (M 2+) with trivalent cations (M 3+)[[22]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352507X20300470#b22).

### 2.5. Zeolites

[Zeolites](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/zeolite "Learn more about Zeolites from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") are crystalline microporous [aluminosilicates](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/aluminosilicate "Learn more about aluminosilicates from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), characterized by normalized spatial arrangement of tetrahedral TO 4 (T = Al, Si), leading to an improved structural arrangement consisting of homogeneous cavities, cages, or 3D networks (usually in the range of 4–15 Å), with maximum and minimum diameters that can easily distinguish molecules with dimensional variation < 1 Å. The replacement of Si IV by Al III in the network makes the whole moiety negatively charged in order to maintain electrical neutrality. The Si/Al ratio in the zeolite frame is related to its ion-exchange capacity and hence, these exhibit ion-exchanging ability and selectivity that make them special materials for electrochemical modifications[[23]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352507X20300470#b23).

### 2.6. Bentonite

Bentonite is a clay mineral that is a phyllosilicate withtetrahedral–octahedral–tetrahedral molecular structure (T–O–T morphology), as shown in [Fig.2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352507X20300470#fig2)[[24]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352507X20300470#b24), whose the mineral composition is primarily dominated by negatively surface charged Na-MMT (smectite group). The common color of bentonites is pale-green, but they do occur with other colors (e.g.,yellow, brown, red, etc.), based on the types of cations present. Bentonites have potential applications in the geo-environmental field to separate pollutants, owing to their low permeable properties (permeate with water)[[25]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352507X20300470#b25), which is dependent on low hydraulic conductivity (κ) of Na-MMT, as it is primary mineral component of bentonite clay[[26]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352507X20300470#b26). It can be used alone or as a modified-natural soil. [geosynthetic](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/geosynthetics "Learn more about geosynthetic from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") clay liners (GCLs, κ= 10−11 m∕s) consist of thin layers of bentonite clay sandwiched between two geo-textiles or strongly connected to a geo-membrane, and are utilized as hydraulic and gas barriers in landfills, contaminated mining waste, and other contaminated sites. GCLs containing bentonite clays have limited thickness, low price, low permeation, very low hydraulic conductivity to distilled water, high swelling nature, capacity of exchangeable cations, ability to withstand different environments, and excellent self-healing characteristics that are adversely affected by the desiccation and cracking in the bentonite[[27]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352507X20300470#b27), [[28]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352507X20300470#b28), [[29]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352507X20300470#b29).

Pure bentonite is unstable to chemicals and reactive in aqueous environments. Thus, high-performance engineered hydraulic barriers (low hydraulic conductivity) used in landfills require frequent inspection of its hydraulic activity, which has hydraulic conductivity parameter. An efficient hydraulic barrier with low values of κ avoids the penetration of water via this hydraulic barrier. Various parameters such as the mineral composition present in bentonite clay, cation locations in the clay, exposure to chemicals, pore fluid, and leakage water containing soluble minerals affect the characteristics [e.g.,hardness, density, surface properties (surface area), wetting nature, swellability, [thixotropy](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/thixotropy "Learn more about thixotropy from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), cation exchange capacity, cohesiveness, sealing, binding with inorganic and organic compounds, etc.] of bentonite. Several types of bentonite are available based on the elemental composition, such as K, Na, Ca, and Al, and the most common types for industrial applications are Na- and Ca-based bentonites. The properties of some of the clays are given in [Table 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352507X20300470#tbl1).

| Chemical composition | Mixture of Al and Si elements | Mainly SiO 2, < 10% of Al 2 O 3 and Fe 2 O 3 | SiO 2, MgO, Al 2 O 3, and inorganic oxides | SiO 2, Na 2 O, Al 2 O 3, K 2 O and other inorganic oxides | NaMMT SBA 15, cloisite, bentonite, attapulgite, kaolinite, natural clay, hyper clay, smectite, kaolins, silicates, etc. | [[30]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352507X20300470#b30), [[31]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352507X20300470#b31), [[32]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352507X20300470#b32) |
| Morphological structure | Nano-tubes | Numerous shapes | Layered structures | Porous structures |  | [[33]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352507X20300470#b33), [[34]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352507X20300470#b34) |
| Size of particle | Length of 0.5 to 2.0 μ m; width 50–200 nm; pore size of 5–30 nm | Length of 5 to 100 μ m; pore size of 0.1 to 1.0 μ m | Length of 5 to 20 μ m, thickness of separate layers: 0.7 to 2.5 nm | Length of few microns, and pore size of 0.5 to 2.0 nm |  |  |
| Specific surface area (m 2/g) | 20 to 150 | 16 to 70 | 10 to 200 | 100 to 900 |  | [[35]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352507X20300470#b35), [[36]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352507X20300470#b36), [[37]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352507X20300470#b37) |

### 2.7. Preparation of clays

Clay minerals (LDHs, MMTs, etc.) are prepared using various strategies such as chemical co-precipitation, ion exchange, reconstruction, and sol–gel processes. Among them, the chemical co-precipitation technique is most frequently used for one-step large-scale direct synthesis of clays (e.g.,LDHs) with a wide range of divalent and trivalent cations and various anions (e.g.,CO 3−, NO 3−, and Br−), and different types of organic dyes and polymers have been intercalated. In this method, both the divalent and trivalent cations along with most of the anions to be intercalated are reacted in an aqueous system, followed by slowly increasing the pH value of the mixed solution through the addition of an alkaline solution (e.g.,NaOH, KOH, etc.). After completion of the chemical co-precipitation reaction, thermal treatment (30–100°C) is performed in order to achieve the maximum yield of clay with excellent crystalline properties. Instead of thermal treatment at low temperature, a high-temperature [hydrothermal method](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/hydrothermal-synthesis "Learn more about hydrothermal method from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") can be employed via heating the sample in an autoclave under high pressure (10–150 MPa) and high temperature (200–1000°C) for a period of a few hours to days.

The ion exchange (indirect process) is also often employed for the synthesis of clays, and has been used for the intercalation of numerous types of anions. In an ion exchange process, LDHs are initially prepared with host common anions (e.g.NO 3−, Br−, SO 4 2−, CO 3−, etc.), and then the anions present in the interlayers are exchanged with the direct anions. Abstract
--------

Composites can be divided into three groups based on their matrix materials, namely polymer, metal and ceramic. Composite materials fail due to micro cracks. Repairing is complex and almost impossible if cracks appear on the surface and interior, which minimizes reliability and material life. In order to save the material from failure and prolong its lifetime without compromising mechanical properties, self-healing is one of the emerging and best techniques. The studies to address the advantages and challenges of self-healing properties of different matrix materials are very limited; however, this review addresses all three different groups of composites. Self-healing composites are fabricated to heal cracks, prevent any obstructed failure, and improve the lifetime of structures. They can self-diagnose their structure after being affected by external forces and repair damages and cracks to a certain degree. This review aims to provide information on the recent developments and prospects of self-healing composites and their applications in various fields such as aerospace, automobiles etc. Fabrication and characterization techniques as well as intrinsic and extrinsic self-healing techniques are discussed based on the latest achievements, including microcapsule embedment, fibers embedment, and vascular networks self-healing.

**Keywords:** microcapsules, hollow fibers, vascular network, healing mechanisms

1. Introduction
---------------

Materials with self-healing capability have gained much attention recently. For example, scratches on a car can be patched or repaired on their own, restoring its original shiny appearance. This is somewhat similar to wound healing in mammals [[1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9740628/#B1-materials-15-08521)]. Structural materials deteriorate and degrade over time leading to micro-cracks that cause failure. Accordingly, to improve the reliability and lifetime of products, repair is necessary [[2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9740628/#B2-materials-15-08521)]. However, it is quite challenging to integrate the healing process of extreme conditions such as fractured bones into manufacturing products due to changing trends in the healing mechanisms of human bodies and other living beings [[3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9740628/#B3-materials-15-08521)].

Self-healing composites are capable of auto-repairing upon crack initiation and regaining their mechanical properties without disturbing the mode of application. Self-healing mechanisms can be divided into two types, extrinsic and intrinsic healing. In extrinsic healing, the healing agent is used as an additive to fill up the cracks in the matrix and in intrinsic healing, a reversible crosslinking bond (supramolecular chemistry) is used to bind the monomers and fill the cracks [[4](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9740628/#B4-materials-15-08521)]. Furthermore, classification can also be made on autonomic healing or non-autonomic healing (i.e., with or without external stimuli). A few well-known methods for developing self-healing composites are the inclusion of microcapsules, hollow fibers, and a vascular network containing healing agents. Self-healing can also be achieved by thermal activation of reversible interactions or dissolved thermoplastic polymers [[5](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9740628/#B5-materials-15-08521)].

The self-healing concept is an emerging technique in the engineering field and it can be applied to composite materials such as polymer matrix composites (PMCs), ceramic matrix composites (CMCs), metal matrix composites (MMCs), and cementitious composites. Apart from the specified areas, the self-healing concept can also be applied to coatings for corrosion protection for commercial applications [[6](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9740628/#B6-materials-15-08521),[7](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9740628/#B7-materials-15-08521)]. Many potential applications of self-healing composites are implemented in resistant fabrics, resealing tires, long-life batteries, aerospace sectors, and automobile fields. In the aerospace sector, the damage due to impact load is severe [[8](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9740628/#B8-materials-15-08521)]. However, self-healing materials can repair damage caused due to outside environment and increase the lifetime of the components. One key application of self-healing materials is to repair dynamic damage and maintain impact resistance [[9](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9740628/#B9-materials-15-08521)].

This study aims to understand the self-healing mechanisms ([Figure 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9740628/#materials-15-08521-f001)) and their techniques which can be applied in day-to-day applications and in emerging fields such as structures, aerospace, automobiles, etc. to improve the life span, and working time and reduce the cost of manufacturing the materials by repairing the existing materials and increase the reliability of materials. Initially, self-healing mechanisms such as microcapsules, hollow fiber embedment, vascular network, and intrinsic healing are explained in subsequent sections. This study also aims to understand the chemistry behind the process of self-healing and implement them in various composites such as polymer matrix composites, metal matrix composites, and ceramic matrix composites to improve their mechanical properties after damage and increase the healing efficiency explained clearly. Finally, self-healing composites in aerospace applications were discussed.

### Figure 1.

[![Image 9: Figure 1](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/b7fd/9740628/b91b18091d9c/materials-15-08521-g001a.jpg)](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/core/lw/2.0/html/tileshop_pmc/tileshop_pmc_inline.html?title=Click%20on%20image%20to%20zoom&p=PMC3&id=9740628_materials-15-08521-g001a.jpg)

Schematic representation of concepts covered in the article. (**a**) Overview of Self-healing mechanisms and chemistry. (**b**) Applying self-healing techniques in various composites such as metals, polymers, and ceramics.

### 1.1. Principle of Self-Healing

The capacity of a material to heal (recover/repair) from damages naturally and autonomously without any external or superficial interference is described as self-healing. Several common words, such as self-repair, autonomic healing, and automatic repair, are implemented in materials to describe their characteristics. Some products also require external intervention to initiate self-healing characteristics [[4](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9740628/#B4-materials-15-08521),[5](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9740628/#B5-materials-15-08521),[6](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9740628/#B6-materials-15-08521)]. Thus, there are two modes of self-healing processes: autonomic (no necessary external intervention) and non-autonomic (needs human triggering or external intervention).

Numerous forms of healing mechanisms are treated in this analysis as, in general, self-healing. The development of cracks in materials reduces the mechanical properties, hence self-healing can be used to recover mechanical characteristics by healing the cracks. Nevertheless, it has been reported that filling and restoring even small pinholes irrespective of cracks is possible to improve the mechanical performance of materials [[7](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9740628/#B7-materials-15-08521),[8](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9740628/#B8-materials-15-08521),[9](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9740628/#B9-materials-15-08521),[10](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9740628/#B10-materials-15-08521)].

#### 1.1.1. Design Strategies

Various material types, such as polymers or plastics, coatings or paints, alloys or metals, and concrete or cementitious alloys, have different systems for self-healing. In this review, various self-healing methods are explored. Strategies have been established with specificity based on different materials and their corresponding characteristics. The design prototype of self-healing components is discussed below:

*   Release of healing agent

*   Reversible crosslinks

*   Miscellaneous technologies such as electro-hydrodynamics, conductivity, shape memory alloy, nanoparticle migration, co-deposition, etc. [[11](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9740628/#B11-materials-15-08521),[12](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9740628/#B12-materials-15-08521),[13](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9740628/#B13-materials-15-08521)].

#### 1.1.2. Release of Healing Agent

Microencapsulation is a mechanism by which micron-sized solid particles or droplets of liquids are sealed in inert shell organs to separate and shield them from outside environments. The idleness is associated with the shell’s reactivity to the encapsulated material. The outcome obtained from the method of microencapsulation was known as microcapsules. To design self-healing polymer composites, microcapsules with catalysts and healing agents are used. [Table 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9740628/#materials-15-08521-t001) shows the factors which are considered while developing microcapsule embedded in the matrix. Early research [[14](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9740628/#B14-materials-15-08521)] shows that employing microencapsulated healings in a matrix of polyester to generate a self-healing action is advised. However, they proved ineffectual in attaining functioning self-healing goods. Maira et al. [[15](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9740628/#B15-materials-15-08521)] performed the first experimental demonstration of self-healing materials by integrating healing agents via encapsulation into the polymer matrix comprising scattered catalysts. The authors used “DCPD” (dicyclopentadiene) as a chemical healing material and a liquid healing agent in their work. As an internal chemical stimulus, Grubb’s catalyst [bis (tricyclohexylphosphine) benzylidene ruthenium (IV) dichloride] and epoxy matrix were used for dispersion since the monomer has high durability, low viscosity, and is comparatively less costly [[16](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9740628/#B16-materials-15-08521),[17](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9740628/#B17-materials-15-08521),[18](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9740628/#B18-materials-15-08521),[19](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9740628/#B19-materials-15-08521),[20](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9740628/#B20-materials-15-08521)].

##### Table 1.

Factors to be considered while developing microcapsule embedded matrix. [[10](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9740628/#B10-materials-15-08521),[11](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9740628/#B11-materials-15-08521),[12](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9740628/#B12-materials-15-08521),[13](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9740628/#B13-materials-15-08521)].

| Agents | Agents Functioning |
| :---: | :---: |
| **Microcapsule** | The healing must be chemically inactive to the polymer shell; the capsule must have a long shelf life. It must be compatible with the dispersed polymer region. The shell walls must be weak to allow for rupture. The catalyst must be close to the capsule. The matrix and the capsule must have a high interfacial attraction. |
| **Monomer Polymerization** | Less volatile for the required time to complete polymerization. No cure-induced shrinkage and stress relaxation. Polymerization at atmospheric temperature |
| **Catalysts** | Dissolves in monomer, clustering does not occur in polymer matrix |
| **Coatings** | Microcapsules have a strong influence on the physical and chemical characteristics of the matrix; Microcapsule’s size will be smaller than the thickness of the coating, and clustering does not occur in the matrix |
| **Healing** | Low economy, must be quicker, multiple cycles |

[Open in a new tab](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9740628/table/materials-15-08521-t001/)

During the development process, liquid ingredients such as dyes, monomers, hardeners, catalysts dyes, and hollow channels are inserted into polymer-generated structures. These pools are ruptured in the event of a fracture, and the reactive agents are poured into fissures by capillary force, where they solidify in the presence of pre-dispersed catalysts to heal the crack (see [Figure 2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9740628/#materials-15-08521-f002)). The primary driver of this process is the formation of cracks. On the other hand, it is challenging to ease the tension from the fracture, which is a massive drawback of this procedure. However, this strategy needs no external input since it is independent. By using this notion of self-healing material design, several options were examined as follows.

##### Figure 2.

[![Image 11: Figure 2](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/b7fd/9740628/c6dca449b33b/materials-15-08521-g002a.jpg)](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/core/lw/2.0/html/tileshop_pmc/tileshop_pmc_inline.html?title=Click%20on%20image%20to%20zoom&p=PMC3&id=9740628_materials-15-08521-g002a.jpg)

[Open in a new tab](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9740628/figure/materials-15-08521-f002/)

Self-healing process using microcapsules [[21](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9740628/#B21-materials-15-08521)].

#### 1.1.3. Microcapsule Embedment

A ring-opening metathesis polymerization (ROMP) occurs, and strongly polymerized metathesis begins as Grubbs catalyst and Dicyclopentadiene (DCPD) are merged. The compounds come into contact when dispersed in an epoxy resin [[22](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9740628/#B22-materials-15-08521),[23](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9740628/#B23-materials-15-08521)]. Tough polycyclopentadiene which is cross-linked can plug the crack as shown in [Figure 3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9740628/#materials-15-08521-f003). Monomer which is low viscous allows it to move across the plane of fracture. According to the experts, fracture regeneration toughness may be increased by 75% compared to the basic specimen [[24](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9740628/#B24-materials-15-08521)].

##### Figure 3.

[![Image 13: Figure 3](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/b7fd/9740628/3db1ad9d56ac/materials-15-08521-g003.jpg)](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/core/lw/2.0/html/tileshop_pmc/tileshop_pmc_inline.html?title=Click%20on%20image%20to%20zoom&p=PMC3&id=9740628_materials-15-08521-g003.jpg)

[Open in a new tab](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9740628/figure/materials-15-08521-f003/)

ROMP (Ring opening metathesis polymerization) of DCPD [[6](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9740628/#B6-materials-15-08521)].

The addition of monomers such as hydroxyl-functionalized polydimethylsiloxane (HOPDMS) and polydiethylsiloxane (PDES) to a synthetic ester base matrix, persists as droplets in microphase separation. The writers utilized encapsulated catalyst instead of a contained monomer healing agent. Inside the matrix, microcapsules of polyurethane with the di-n-dibutyltin dilaurate (DBTL) catalyst are then eliminated [[25](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9740628/#B25-materials-15-08521),[26](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9740628/#B26-materials-15-08521),[27](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9740628/#B27-materials-15-08521),[28](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9740628/#B28-materials-15-08521),[29](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9740628/#B29-materials-15-08521)]. The catalyst reacts with the monomer whenever these capsules burst, thus, polycondensation happens. Researchers created self-healing elastomers on polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) by combining two types of micro-sized capsules: a resin and an initiator capsule. References [[30](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9740628/#B30-materials-15-08521),[31](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9740628/#B31-materials-15-08521),[32](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9740628/#B32-materials-15-08521)] looked at the efficiency of tiny capsules in self-recovery. The authors recently posted the creation of self–healing polymer composites without catalysts. Numerous research is being conducted in this area and it is undoubtedly complex due to these discoveries. The authors recently demonstrated using latent curing, CuBr2 (2-Melm) 4, rather than a solid stage, to produce self-mending materials via ROMP reaction.

#### 1.1.4. Hollow Fiber Embedment

The self-healing solution based on microcapsules has the main drawback of instability in resulting in thorough and multiple healing since it consists of a small supply of healing agents. Multiple healing is only possible if there is the existence of overheating agent in the matrix after the initial healing. As a result, a new reservoir capable of delivering larger quantities of liquid healing agents to attain multiple healing in composite materials has been developed [[33](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9740628/#B33-materials-15-08521)]. However, they have only had little success with their technique. Broad-diameter capillaries were later placed in resins [[34](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9740628/#B34-materials-15-08521)], but the trials were unsuccessful. Smaller hollow glass fibers, also known as Hollex fibers, were placed in resins [[35](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9740628/#B35-materials-15-08521)]. However, composites formulated based on glass-reinforced fibers failed to produce healing in the crack. Strong viscous resins were not suitable and curing was terrible.

Chen et al. [[36](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9740628/#B36-materials-15-08521)] developed a method for optimizing the manufacturing of hollow fiber glasses to be used as a liquid curing agent and dye container. Borosilicate glass fiber has a diameter of 30 to 100 micrometers and a 55% hollow ([Figure 4](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9740628/#materials-15-08521-f004)). They prepared hollow fibers using composite panels and recovered up to 97% of the original flexural power by containing repair agents [[37](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9740628/#B37-materials-15-08521),[38](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9740628/#B38-materials-15-08521),[39](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9740628/#B39-materials-15-08521),[40](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9740628/#B40-materials-15-08521),[41](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9740628/#B41-materials-15-08521),[42](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9740628/#B42-materials-15-08521)]. Additionally, the authors reported the penetration of fluorescent dye along broken hollow fibers into the crack plane. Having these self-healing material construction techniques provides some benefits, which are as follows:

*   To restore injury, a more considerable amount of curing agent should be available.

*   Various activation methods/resin forms can be chosen.

*   Visual examination of the affected region is possible.

*   Hollow fibers can be quickly combined and adapted with traditional reinforcing fibers.

Self-healing of Hollow fibers [[21](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9740628/#B21-materials-15-08521)].

#### 1.1.5. Micro Vascular Systems

Another strategy analogous to the natural vascular system of several flowers and animals was investigated to resolve the restricted method of inserting a healing agent into the microcapsule [[43](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9740628/#B43-materials-15-08521),[44](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9740628/#B44-materials-15-08521)]. This technology is dependent on a community with a continuously centralized administration of healing solutions to the polymer structure.

The production procedure is sophisticated for such networks for practical applications, and it is not easy to make synthetic materials. enriched_points.expanded_content: Conduct a comprehensive literature review to identify existing research on self-healing polymers, including synthesis methods, healing mechanisms, and applications across various materials.
Utilize controlled polymerization techniques, such as RAFT or ATRP, to create polymers with tailored functionalities that facilitate self-healing through reversible bonding or encapsulation.
Assess the healing efficiency through standardized testing methods, analyzing mechanical properties and structural integrity before and after healing to determine the effectiveness of self-healing.
